course_description: This course is designed to introduce students to social science research methods that are relevant within tourism, leisure and associated fields. Students will develop an understanding of why research is conducted and will be introduced to basic designs, models and processes that are applicable in tourism and leisure research. As a part of this course, students will be given a basic introduction to quantitative and qualitative methods of description and measurement.
timetable:
Timetables are available on mySI-net.

Next 2. Aims
course_aims: This course aims to build knowledge and skills that will allow students to design and implement research strategies, and to understand, critically evaluate and apply research findings in the context of tourism, hospitality and leisure.

learning_outcomes.description: understand the process of research and its application in the tourism, hospitality and leisure sectors
recognise the assumptions underlying different research approaches and methodologies
formulate research questions, aims and/or objectives in order to extend knowledge or improve practice
select and adequately justify appropriate methods to investigate research problems
understand the basic principles concerning data analysis and potential data analysis tools
understand the appropriate level, style and means of communication to report research findings
critically evaluate the design, implementation and analysis of research

assessment_details.title: Individual Multiple Choice Quiz
assessment_details.task_description: This assessment comprises a multiple choice quiz which is to be conducted in class. The quiz will comprise 30 multiple choice questions covering the lecture material and course readings for the first 5 weeks. Each correctly answered question is worth 1 mark.

assessment_details.title: Individual Article Critique
assessment_details.task_description:
Students need to locate an empirical research article that has been published in an academic journal and undertake a critique of the design, implementation, analysis and presentation of the research outlined in that article. Students can select an article that reports either a quantitative piece of research, a qualitative piece of research or a mixed method piece of research.

Students are expected to undertake a critical review of the research outlined in the article and demonstrate they understand the aspects which make research valid and reliable. Students are encouraged to use the checklists provided in class and in BlackBoard during the session 'Evaluating quantitative and qualitative research' to assist with the article critique.

Please limit your article review to no more than 1,200 words

learning_resources.required_resources: Veal, A.J. 2006. Research Methods for Leisure and Tourism: A Practical Guide . London : Pearson Education Limited.
learning_resources.recommended_resources: Ritchie, B.W., Burns, P. & Palmer, C. 2005. Tourism Research Methods: Integrating Theory with Practice. Wallingford: CAB International.
learning_resources.university_learning_resources:
Access to required and recommended resources, plus past central exam papers, is available at the UQ Library website ( http://library.uq.edu.au/search/r?SEARCH=TOUR7000 ).

The University offers a range of resources and services to support student learning. Details are available on the myServices website ( https://student.my.uq.edu.au/ ).

Previous 2. Aims

Next 4. Activities

learning_activities.topic: Course Introduction and Overview (Lecture): This session will provide an introduction and overview of the course including an outline of the importance, value and types of research. Readings/Ref: Ritchie (2005) (Chapter 1); Veal (2006) (Chapters 1-2)

Research Planning and Proposals (Lecture): This session will provide an overview of the main steps in research planning and implementation. It will also cover the sections of a research proposal (vital for your assessment task). Readings/Ref: Veal (2006) (Chapter 3)

Literature Reviews and Topic Selection (Lecture): This session will outline the role of a literature review in research planning and provide tips to refine research topic selection, develop research questions, aims and objectives. Readings/Ref: Veal (2006) (Chapter 5)

Research Approaches and Measurement (Lecture): This session outlines the range of research philosophies and approaches researchers can take and how they influence the choice of research methodology, methods and analysis. It will also discuss the issue of validity and reliability in research. Readings/Ref: Ritchie (2005) (Chapters 3, 4); Veal (2006) (Chapter 4)

Qualitative Research Designs and Methods (Lecture): This session will provide an introduction to the qualitative research methodology, available methods and ways to overcome possible issues in qualitative data collection.

Individual In Class Quiz (Quiz): You will sit an in class multi-choice quiz which is worth 30% of your final marks. It will comprise 30 questions from the first 5 weeks of course materials (including readings). More details can be found on the assessment section of the ECP.

Survey Research and Sampling (Lecture): This session will provide an introduction to the most common quantitative method (survey research) and how to design and implement surveys. Readings/Ref: Ritchie (2005) (Chapters 11, 16); Veal (2006) (Chapters 9 and 10)

Experimental Design and Hypothesis Testing (Lecture): This session will provide an introduction to experimental design and the role of hypothesis testing in research. Some of the advantages and disadvantages of these approaches will also be outlined.

Data Analysis Tools and Presentation (Lecture): This session will provide an introduction to the range of data analysis tools and how data should be presented and communicated to different audiences, depending on the nature of the data and the purpose of the research. Readings/Ref: Ritchie (2005) (chapter 15); Veal (2006) (Chapters 11, 12, 13)

Evaluating Qualitative and Quantitative Research (Lecture): This session will outline the key questions to ask in evaluating the quality of research. This will help with your article critique and bring aspects of the course together.
Guest Lecture Case Studies (Lecture): This session will provide students with examples of research design, implementation and analysis from staff and student research projects.
Guest Lecture Case Studies (Lecture): This session will provide students with examples of research design, implementation and analysis from staff and student research projects.
Conclusion and Evaluation (Lecture): This session will provide a conclusion to the course; consider future research directions and approaches; and undertake a course evaluation.

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the Universitys assessment-related policies can be found on myAdvisor ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2910 ).

Academic Integrity
It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Plagiarism
The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Academic Integrity and Plagiarism policy ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25128 ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Feedback on Assessment
Feedback is essential to effective learning and students can expect to receive appropriate and timely feedback on all assessment. For a detailed explanation of the feedback you are entitled to, you should consult the policy on Student Access to Feedback on Assessment. ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25109 )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the myAdvisor web site. ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2953 )

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses
Students on a placement course also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice should refer to the University policy, Placement Courses ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25120 ) for detailed information.

Working with Children
Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25004 ) to find out how to apply for a blue card.

Students with a Disability
Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Adviser at Student Support Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25122 ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25111

While it is the responsibility of the relevant faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of any adjustment to an academic program, the University Health Service can arrange appropriate advice and assistance on personal and public health issues.

Occupational Health and Safety
Undergraduate Students ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25055 ) and Postgraduate Students ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25057 ) should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory.

Previous 5. Assessment

Next Summary
course_introduction:
Students in this course will be given a basic introduction to research design, implementation and analysis, with a particular emphasis on the methods most commonly used in tourism, leisure and hospitality research. In class excerises coupled with guest lectures will equip students to undertake basic research procedures and to understand the issues and problems in undertaking research. Students will also develop a critical approach to designing and implementing research methods and an ability to justify choosing particular research approaches.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

graduate_attributes.intro: Successfully completing this course will contribute to the recognition of your attainment of the following UQ (Postgrad Coursework) graduate attributes:

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A2 . A broad understanding of the field of study, including how other disciplines relate to the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A3 . A comprehensive and in-depth knowledge in the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A5 . An international perspective on the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A7 . An appreciation of the link between theory and practice .
B1 . The ability to collect , analyse and organise information and ideas and to convey those ideas clearly and fluently , in both written and spoken forms .
B2 . The ability to interact effectively with others in order to work towards a common outcome.
B3 . The ability to select and use the appropriate level , style and means of communication .
B4 . The ability to engage effectively and appropriately with information and communication technologies .
B5 . The ability to practise as part of an interdisciplinary team .
C2 . The ability to work and learn independently and effectively .
C3 . The ability to generate ideas and adapt innovatively to changing environments.
C5 . The ability to formulate and investigate problems , create solutions , innovate and improve current practices .
C6 . The abilities and skills that provide a foundation for future leadership roles.
D2 . The ability to apply critical reasoning to issues through independent thought and informed judgement .
D4 . The ability to process material and to critically analyse and integrate information from a wide range of sources.
D5 . The ability to evaluate opinions , make decisions and to reflect critically on the justifications for decisions using an evidence-based approach .
E1 . An understanding of social and civic responsibility .
E3 . An appreciation of the philosophical and social contexts of a discipline.
E4 . A knowledge and respect of ethics and ethical standards in relation to a major area of study.
E5 . A knowledge of other cultures and times and an appreciation of cultural diversity .
E7 . The ability to work effectively and sensitively across all areas of society .
E8 . An understanding of and respect for the roles and expertise of associated disciplines .

other_learning_activities_information:
Learning materials will be presented via a two-hour lecture session each week. These sessions will provide value information from the lecturer, group discussion and small-group exercises, as well as guest lecture case studies. Students are also encouraged to read widely regarding the course topics, using either the recommended texts or other similar texts. Students are expected to attend all scheduled classes and should note that the assessment items for the course are directly linked to class sessions.

Previous 3. Resources

Next 5. Assessment
course_grading:
Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Fail: Fails to demonstrate most or all of the basic requirements of the course:

1% - 29%

Grade 2, Fail: Demonstrates clear deficiencies in understanding and applying fundamental concepts; communicates information or ideas in ways that are frequently incomplete or confusing and give little attention to the conventions of the discipline:

30% - 46%

Grade 3, Fail: Demonstrates superficial or partial or faulty understanding of the fundamental concepts of the field of study and limited ability to apply these concepts; presents undeveloped or inappropriate or unsupported arguments; communicates information or ideas with lack of clarity and inconsistent adherence to the conventions of the discipline:

47% - 49%

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrates adequate understanding and application of the fundamental concepts of the field of study; develops routine arguments or decisions and provides acceptable justification; communicates information and ideas adequately in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

50% - 64%

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrates substantial understanding of fundamental concepts of the field of study and ability to apply these concepts in a variety of contexts; develops or adapts convincing arguments and provides coherent justification; communicates information and ideas clearly and fluently in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

65% - 74%

Grade 6, Distinction: As for 5, with frequent evidence of originality in defining and analysing issues or problems and in creating solutions; uses a level, style and means of communication appropriate to the discipline and the audience:

75% - 84%

Grade 7, High Distinction: As for 6, with consistent evidence of substantial originality and insight in identifying, generating and communicating competing arguments, perspectives or problem solving approaches; critically evaluates problems, their solutions and implications:

85% - 100%
late_submission:
Requests for the granting of extensions must be made in writing (or via email) to the Course Coordinator at least 24 hours prior to the submission date. An extension will only be considered if the request is supported by relevant documentation that clearly outlines a medical or other extenuating circumstance. If an extension is approved, students will be informed in writing and this evidence must be attached to the assignment upon submission.

Items submitted after the due date and time (for which no extension has been granted), incur a late submission penalty. The penalty is at the rate of 10% of the total available marks for that particular piece of assessment, for each working day or part thereof that the item is overdue up to a maximum of 10 days. Weekends are treated as one day (i.e. if an assignment is due on a Friday, submission on the following Monday would be considered 2 days late and incur a deduction of 20%).

Assignments received more than 10 days after the due date will not be accepted and will be returned ungraded, unless prior arrangements for extension have been made.
other_assessment_information:
St Lucia Campus students:

Unless otherwise specified all assignments must be lodged by the due date and time stated in the course profile, and are to be submitted through the BEL Faculty Collaborative Learning Centre (FCLC) located on level 1 of the Colin Clark Building.

1. Each assignment must be accompanied by the appropriate, fully completed FCLC Tourism Assignment Cover Sheet, available online from www.bel.uq.edu.au or the FCLC.

2. Assignments must not be faxed or emailed to the Course Coordinator without prior arrangement.

3. All assignments will be stamped with the date of receipt, or if submitted after hours with the date of the NEXT business day.

4. Students must sign the cover sheet, acknowledging the plagiarism Statute of the University and must always keep a copy of their work.

5. All group assignments must have ONE nominated representatives details at the top of the coversheet, with details of other group members listed on the lower section.

6. Students should keep a list of their group members' full names and contact details.

7. Please Note: Students must produce their student ID card (or drivers license) to collect marked assignments.

Note: Submit your assignment at the Campus where you are enrolled.
